description: Figure 1 presents log–log plots of weight gain versus time for high-purity Fe–Mn–Al alloys isothermally oxidized at 800°C in pure oxygen (200 torr) for up to 24 hr. Panel (a) shows alloys that form protective alumina scales, with composition-labeled curves (e.g., 10–9, 30–11, 5–8, 20–10, 1–8) exhibiting near-linear behavior; the fitted slopes give exponent values n = 2.9, 3.7, 10.6, 4.6, and 5.6 from the rate equation (ΔW/A)^n = kt, indicating a wide kinetic range rather than simple parabolic oxidation. Panel (b) displays alloys exhibiting breakaway and healing kinetics (e.g., 25–7, 5–6, 7.5–5(58), 20–6(37), 25–6, 1–6), where weight-gain curves are segmented: an initial slow-growth stage is followed by a sharp acceleration (breakaway) and subsequent deceleration (healing), reflecting cyclic failure and reformation of the oxide scale. The figure contrasts stable, low-rate protective oxidation with unstable, high-rate non-protective behavior, supporting the conclusion that oxidation resistance in Fe–Mn–Al alloys is strongly composition-dependent and that simple rate laws poorly describe the breakaway–healing regime.

This figure comprises two composition–property line graphs reporting the isothermal oxidation weight gains of high-purity Fe–Mn–Al alloys after 24 h at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. Panel (a) plots 24 h weight gain (ΔW/24, mg cm⁻²) versus manganese concentration (0–30 wt%) for alloys with fixed aluminum contents labeled 6AL, 7AL, 8AL, and 9AL. The curves show that additions of less than about 2 wt% Mn sharply increase oxidation rates relative to binary Fe–Al alloys, whereas minima near 5–10 wt% Mn correspond to significantly reduced weight gains before rates rise again at higher Mn levels. Panel (b) plots ΔW/24 versus aluminum concentration (5–17.5 wt%) for fixed manganese levels (2.5, 5, 10, 15, and 20 Mn, per the legend). Distinct minima are observed at approximately 6–9 wt% Al for each Mn level; beyond about 9 wt% Al, weight gains steadily increase, an effect attributed to the growth of small manganese-rich oxide nodules through the alumina scale. Collectively, the panels establish that the most oxidation-resistant compositions lie within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, where continuous protective alumina scales form.

This figure presents the ternary oxide map for the Fe–Mn–Al system at 800°C, superimposing isothermal phase boundaries and oxidation scale morphology regions as a function of aluminum (0–25 wt.%, left axis) and manganese (0–40 wt.%, bottom axis) content. The diagram delineates alloy phase fields including ferrite (α), austenite (γ), α+γ duplex, and α+β-Mn regions, alongside four distinct scale morphology zones labeled I through IV. Region I corresponds to non-protective alumina interspersed with bulky oxide nodules at low aluminum levels (<6 wt.%). Region II covers duplex α+γ alloys exhibiting alumina scales with bulky manganese-rich oxides and wormlike threads, indicating poor oxidation resistance. Region III, situated within the fully ferritic field around Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, represents the most oxidation-resistant compositions that develop continuous protective alumina scales. Region IV, at higher aluminum (>9 wt.%), shows alumina scales penetrated by small manganese oxide nodules. The map visually establishes that the optimum oxidation resistance is restricted to the ferritic Region III, whereas the presence of austenite is detrimental.

Figure 4 shows a metallographic cross-section of a large oxide nodule formed on an Fe-Mn-Al alloy after isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hr. The bright white region at the lower left is the alloy substrate, from which a bulky, irregular multilayered nodule protrudes upward into the dark mounting medium. The nodule displays distinct contrast bands indicating layered oxide growth. According to the caption, the alloy composition is Fe-7.5Mn-3Al, whereas the main text refers to Fe-7.5Mn-5Al for this micrograph. The cited text reports that the nodule consists of a white outer Fe2O3 layer and an inner (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer, with significant manganese and aluminum enrichment occurring in the inner layer and at the gas-oxide interface. Dark patches visible within the nodule suggest porosity or localized phase variations. This image illustrates the non-protective oxidation morphology characteristic of alloys with insufficient aluminum to form a continuous alumina scale, supporting the finding that ~7.5% Mn alloys develop large iron-rich nodules structurally akin to those on binary Fe-Al alloys.

Figure 5 comprises two complementary panels characterizing an oxide nodule on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al. The left panel is an X-ray dot map showing elemental distribution within the nodule, with bright regions indicating localized enrichment; the specific element is unreadable from the image alone. The right panel is a scanning electron micrograph of a polished cross-section revealing a bilayered scale adjacent to the metallic substrate on the right: a bright outer layer, a darker inner layer containing pores or inclusions, and a relatively flat alloy–oxide interface. The caption identifies these as a scanning electron micrograph and X-ray images showing elemental distribution within the nodule. Citation sentences report that manganese and aluminum are highly enriched in the Fe3O4 inner layer and at the gas–oxide interface, corresponding to an external thin grey semicontinuous manganese oxide layer. This figure provides microstructural evidence that Mn and Al segregate to the inner oxide and outer surface during nodule growth, supporting the proposed mechanism of manganese-rich oxide formation on Fe–Mn–Al alloys that contain insufficient aluminum to maintain a fully protective alumina scale.

This X-ray elemental mapping image (Fig. 5, continued) presents the spatial distribution of enriched elements within the cross-section of a large oxide nodule formed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al alloy after isothermal oxidation at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. As reported in the accompanying text, the X-ray images reveal a high degree of enrichment of manganese and aluminum in the inner Fe₃O₄ layer and specifically at the gas-oxide interface of the nodule structure. The bright contrast regions correspond to areas of elemental enrichment, supporting the proposed multilayered nodule architecture comprising an outer white Fe₂O₃ layer and an inner Fe₃O₄ layer. The observed manganese enrichment at the gas-oxide interface correlates with the formation of a thin grey semicontinuous manganese oxide outer layer. This elemental distribution evidence underpins the conclusion that alloy compositions with approximately 7.5% manganese represent a transition boundary in oxidation behavior, where nodule morphology shifts from large iron-rich to smaller manganese-rich structures, and it confirms the critical role of localized Mn and Al segregation in determining scale microstructure and non-protective oxidation characteristics.

This figure presents a grayscale X-ray elemental distribution map, constituting a continued panel of Figure 5, showing the cross-section of an oxide nodule or scale formed on an Fe–7.5Mn–5Al alloy after high-temperature oxidation. The image displays a curved layered structure with a bright outer rim and variable contrast across the inner region, consistent with the bilayer oxide morphology described in the text. According to the accompanying citation, Fig. 5 comprises X-ray images revealing significant enrichment of manganese and aluminum within the Fe3O4 inner layer and at the gas-oxide interface following isothermal exposure at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. Manganese enrichment at the gas-oxide interface is specifically associated with an outer thin grey semicontinuous oxide layer for alloys containing more than about 5% manganese. The figure supports the core conclusion that oxide scales on Fe–Mn–Al alloys exhibit pronounced compositional stratification, with manganese and aluminum partitioning into distinct layers, thereby governing the transition from iron-oxide-dominated nodules toward manganese-rich oxide morphologies as the bulk manganese concentration increases.

The scanning electron micrograph shows the surface morphology of a Fe-7.5Mn-5Al alloy after isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hours in pure oxygen at 200 torr, displaying both large and small oxide nodules. The field of view is dominated by a central large, cauliflower-like nodule with a rough textured surface, surrounded by numerous smaller spherical nodules dispersed across the oxidized surface. The scale bar indicates 130 μm. According to the caption and cited text, this alloy composition lies at the critical transition boundary near 7.5% manganese, where both nodular types coexist. The large nodules are reported to comprise an outer Fe2O3 layer and an inner (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer, while the small nodules possess a grey manganese oxide layer at the gas-oxide interface above a white Fe2O3 layer. This figure provides direct visual evidence that the transition from large iron-rich nodules to small manganese-rich nodules occurs at approximately 7.5% Mn, supporting the core conclusion that manganese concentration governs the scale morphology and the shift from iron-dominated to manganese-dominated oxidation behavior.

This cross-sectional micrograph displays the morphology of small oxide nodules formed on the Fe–10Mn–5Al alloy after 24 hr of isothermal oxidation at 800°C in pure oxygen. The image reveals several nodules situated along the interface between the upper dark region and the lower light-colored alloy substrate, with a 7 μm scale bar. The caption and accompanying text report that these nodules represent the small-nodule morphology typical of alloys containing more than ~7.5% Mn, marking a transition from large iron-rich nodules. The reported nodule structure comprises a grey manganese oxide (Mn₂O₃) layer at the gas–oxide interface overlying a white Fe₂O₃ layer, which contains particles of an aluminum-rich phase and is underlain by a continuous aluminum-rich layer at the nodule–alloy interface. This micrograph supports the key finding that manganese concentration governs oxide nodule morphology and that small Mn-rich nodules form on higher-Mn Fe–Mn–Al alloys under these oxidation conditions.

This figure presents paired micrographs of the Fe-15Mn-6Al alloy after isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hr in pure oxygen at 200 torr, illustrating the relationship between substrate microstructure and oxide morphology. The left subfigure is a cross-sectional view showing the alloy/scale interface, where bulky manganese-rich oxide formations are visible growing directly above precipitated austenite plates within the duplex ferrite-austenite substrate. The right subfigure is a surface SEM micrograph revealing a wormlike morphology consisting of fine manganese oxide threads that have grown along grain boundaries and at distinct orientations on grain surfaces. Together, these images demonstrate that the scale morphology is intimately related to the underlying substrate structure, with austenite acting as preferential nucleation sites for non-protective, bulky manganese-rich oxides. The micrographs provide direct visual evidence that austenite is detrimental to oxidation resistance by promoting breakdown of the preexisting alumina scale, supporting the paper's central conclusion that fully ferritic alloys in the Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al range form continuous protective alumina whereas duplex structures suffer localized breakaway oxidation.

This micrograph presents a metallographic cross-section of the duplex Fe–20Mn–6Al alloy following isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hr in pure oxygen at 200 torr. The image reveals a stratified scale morphology characteristic of Region II behavior: bulky manganese-rich oxides dominate the upper dark scale layer, while a light-etching ferrite surface layer lies directly beneath the oxide–metal interface, indicating austenite depletion in the substrate immediately below the scale. The underlying metal microstructure shows clearly resolved grain and phase boundaries. The 20 μm scale bar confirms the micron-scale dimensions of the ferrite layer and oxide protrusions. As reported in the accompanying text, this morphology exemplifies how austenite is detrimental to oxidation resistance in Fe–Mn–Al alloys, promoting breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and growth of bulky manganese-rich oxides above secondary ferrite layers that form during high-temperature exposure.

Scanning electron micrographs showing manganese oxide threads on the surfaces of (a) Fe-20Mn-8Al and (b) Fe-30Mn-8Al after 24 hr oxidation at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. Subfigure (a) displays fine, wormlike manganese oxide threads distributed across the oxidized surface, which the authors associate with a relatively high ferrite content, noting that such threads grew above grain boundaries and at distinct orientations on grain surfaces. Subfigure (b) exhibits more prominent wormlike oxide threads together with bulky manganese-rich oxides; the authors report that these morphologies appeared to grow through the alumina scale and are characteristic of alloys containing higher proportions of austenite. Both micrographs reveal thread-like oxide formations directly linked to substrate structure. The comparison supports the core conclusion that austenite promotes the breakdown of preexisting protective alumina scales and stimulates the growth of manganese-rich oxide threads, whereas optimal oxidation resistance occurs in fully ferritic alloys within the Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al composition range that develop continuous alumina scales.

This scanning electron micrograph presents three surface views of Fe-Mn-Al alloys after isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hr in pure oxygen at 200 torr, specifically illustrating areas of spalled alumina and void formation. Subfigure (a) shows Fe-25Mn-8Al exhibiting angular voids beneath spalled regions, characteristic of region II duplex alloys where austenite promotes alumina scale breakdown. Subfigure (b) depicts Fe-30Mn-9Al with smooth, rounded voids, which the text notes form on all alloys containing more than 25% manganese regardless of aluminum concentration. Subfigure (c) reveals Fe-30Mn-12Al where numerous smooth rounded voids appear filled or partially filled with manganese oxide nodules. Together, these micrographs demonstrate a compositional transition in void morphology from angular to rounded as manganese content increases above 25%, and provide direct visual evidence supporting the proposed mechanism of manganese oxide nodule nucleation and growth via manganese vapor reaction within voids beneath the alumina scale.

This scanning electron micrograph displays the surface morphology of oxide protrusions formed on Fe-15Mn-8Al alloy following isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hr in pure oxygen at 200 torr. The micrograph reveals a dense and relatively uniform population of small nodular protrusions distributed across the oxidized surface, which the accompanying text reports are typically less than one micron in diameter. Unlike the larger bulky oxides characteristic of region II alloys, the caption and discussion state that their spatial distribution is not structurally related to the underlying alloy microstructure. The text further notes that few such protrusions are observed on low-manganese alloys, whereas they become abundant at high manganese concentrations, as clearly illustrated by this Fe-15Mn-8Al specimen. These small manganese-rich oxide features represent a distinct morphological component associated with protective alumina-forming regions, supporting the core conclusion that elevated manganese contents promote the nucleation and growth of fine surface oxide outgrowths on otherwise protective scales.

Figure 13 presents an X-ray elemental distribution map, as stated in the caption, acquired from a small oxide nodule formed on Fe-15Mn-13Al (run 11) after oxidation at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. The grayscale contrast reveals spatial variation in X-ray intensity across the nodule, reflecting heterogeneous elemental distribution within the penetrated alumina scale. The alloy composition places it in Region IV (Al > 9%), where alumina scales are breached by small manganese-rich oxide nodules. The caption and text report that these nodules contain one or two manganese oxide phases and a thin dark aluminum-rich oxide layer at the nodule-alloy interface. While the specific element mapped in this single panel is not visually labeled, the image demonstrates the localized enrichment characteristic of such nodules. This micrograph supports the conclusion that excessive aluminum promotes the growth of manganese oxide nodules through otherwise protective alumina scales, compromising oxidation resistance.

This figure presents a paired micrograph and elemental map set from Figure 13, showing cross-sectional morphology and compositional distribution associated with Region IV in the Fe-Mn-Al oxidation map (aluminum >9 wt.%). The left image is an SEM micrograph of an alloy oxidized at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr, revealing a small oxide nodule that has penetrated through the alumina scale; a thin, darker aluminum-rich oxide layer is visible at the nodule-alloy interface. The right image is the corresponding X-ray elemental distribution map, which the paper reports was obtained by electron-probe microanalysis; bright dot clusters are concentrated within the nodule region, and the accompanying text identifies these nodules as being composed of one or two manganese oxide phases, confirming localized manganese enrichment. Together, the images provide direct microstructural and compositional evidence that alloys with aluminum levels exceeding 9% form alumina scales which are locally breached by penetrating manganese-rich oxide nodules, thereby compromising scale protectivity and supporting the paper’s broader conclusion that the most oxidation-resistant compositions lie within the fully ferritic Fe–(5–10)%Mn–(6–10)%Al range.

Figure 14 comprises three scanning electron micrographs showing the surface morphology of Fe-10Mn-6Al alloy after isothermal oxidation for 100 hr in pure oxygen at 200 torr, comparing oxide scale evolution at 800°C (a) and 1000°C (b). The micrograph representing the 800°C condition exhibits localized surface degradation with lifting, buckling, and growth of small bulky oxide nodules at discrete sites. The 1000°C images reveal thick, convoluted alumina scales with pronounced cracking and layered fracture features; caption states this scale is much thicker than at 600 or 800°C and consists of two distinct layers, adhering to the substrate except at specimen edges. One micrograph displays deep convolutions and a through-scale crack consistent with a thick duplex alumina layer, while another shows ring-like surface textures across the oxidized surface. Together, these images demonstrate that increasing the oxidation temperature from 800 to 1000°C transforms the scale from locally degraded alumina with bulky oxides to an extensive, thick convoluted alumina structure, supporting the conclusion that this alloy composition develops protective alumina at 800°C but undergoes severe scale thickening and morphological convolution at 1000°C.

Figure 15 presents two scanning electron micrographs showing the surface morphologies of Fe–7.5Mn–4Al–5Cr alloy after isothermal oxidation at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. Subfigure (a) shows a hollow, convoluted oxide mound formed after 24 hr, exhibiting a highly porous and cellular internal structure with numerous voids and cavities. Subfigure (b) shows the surface after 100 hr oxidation, revealing oxide threads adjacent to spalled alumina regions, together with numerous small manganese-rich oxide nodules. According to the caption and text, this alloy contains 4% aluminum—below the threshold required to form continuous protective alumina scales in this system—and therefore develops non-protective hollow oxide mounds. After 100 hr, all alloys exhibited scale degradation; the presence of oxide threads and spalled alumina indicates breakdown of the initially formed scale. Although small chromium additions generally reduce the aluminum concentration needed for protective alumina formation, this particular composition still suffers from scale degradation over extended exposure, with manganese-rich oxide nodules and threads marking the failure of long-term protection.

This figure comprises two micrographs of the scale formed on Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr after isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 hr in pure oxygen at 200 torr. The left image (Fig. 16b) is an X-ray distribution map revealing manganese enrichment, where brighter contrast marks higher manganese concentration within the scale. The right image (Fig. 16a) is a cross-sectional micrograph showing an extremely convoluted, wavy oxide scale that appears partially detached from the underlying substrate. The scale exhibits highly uneven thickness, and citation states the substrate surface became totally ferritic and manganese-depleted, while aluminum enriched at the scale base. The images collectively demonstrate that thicker scale sections are highly enriched in manganese and that the convoluted morphology correlates with duplex alloy breakdown, supporting the conclusion that austenite precipitates promote failure of protective alumina scales and growth of bulky manganese-rich oxides in Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys.

This figure presents schematic cross-sectional illustrations of oxide nodules formed on Fe-Mn-Al alloys oxidized at 800°C, delineating three distinct structural types dependent on manganese concentration. Type 1 depicts large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys containing less than about 5% manganese, consisting of an outer Fe2O3 layer, an inner (Fe,Mn)3O4 region, and internal oxide particles of (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 or Al2O3. Type 2 shows large iron oxide nodules on alloys with approximately 5–7.5% manganese, featuring discontinuous Mn2O3 layers or particles at the gas-oxide interface, a Fe2O3 layer, a partial healing layer at the nodule base, and underlying (Fe,Mn)3O4 with (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 or Al2O3. Type 3 illustrates small manganese-rich nodules on alloys containing at least 7.5% manganese, characterized by a continuous outer Mn2O3 layer, an intermediate Fe2O3 layer, and a complete healing layer of (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 or Al2O3 at the metal-oxide interface. The progression demonstrates that increasing manganese content transforms large iron-rich nodules into smaller manganese-rich ones, promotes the formation of continuous Mn2O3 and aluminate layers, and enables complete healing layer formation, thereby retarding further oxidation by limiting ionic diffusion.

This figure presents a six-panel schematic illustration depicting the sequential stages of bulky manganese-rich oxide growth during the high-temperature oxidation of duplex α+γ Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. The first panel shows the initial ferritic (α) Fe-Mn-Al matrix. The second panel depicts the formation of a continuous protective Al₂O₃ scale over the α phase. The third panel illustrates the nucleation and growth of austenite (γ) plates beneath the alumina scale, causing local deformation of the metal-scale interface. The fourth panel, labeled "Stage 3," shows the outward growth of wormlike Mn₂O₃ oxide threads through weaknesses in the overlying alumina scale. The fifth panel presents a hemispherical bulky manganese-rich oxide nodule that has incorporated the oxide threads and alumina scale into its growing structure. The sixth panel provides a detailed cross-sectional view of the layered bulky oxide, explicitly labeling an outer Mn₂O₃ layer and an inner Fe₂O₃ layer above the alloy substrate containing both α and γ phases. The progression visualizes the mechanism by which austenite is detrimental to oxidation resistance: it promotes breakdown of the preexisting protective alumina scale and enables rapid growth of bulky Mn-rich oxides, accompanied by manganese depletion and conversion of austenite back to ferrite beneath the oxide nodule.

Figure 19 comprises two panels illustrating the oxide scale structures formed on high-manganese Fe–Mn–Al alloys during high-temperature oxidation. Panel (a) is a cross-sectional micrograph of the scale formed on Fe–25Mn–7Al after 24 hr at 800 °C in pure oxygen at 200 torr, revealing a convoluted metal-oxide interface with seven labeled oxide nodules or regions. The visible phases include Mn3O4, (Fe,Mn)3O4, Mn2O3, and (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 distributed across the scale thickness, and a 10 μm scale bar is provided. Panel (b) presents a schematic representation summarizing three characteristic oxide structures (Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3) observed on Fe–Mn–Al alloys containing more than 20% manganese. Type 1 features a Mn3O4 core surrounded by Mn2O3 with an underlying (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer and incomplete (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 interfacial layers; Type 2 shows separate upper Mn2O3 and lower Mn3O4 regions with Al2O3 and incomplete (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal interface; Type 3 consists of Mn2O3 over a complete (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 barrier layer. Together, the figure demonstrates that the bulky manganese-rich scales on these alloys comprise layered manganese oxides and iron-manganese spinels, and that the presence and completeness of interfacial aluminate layers dictate whether Mn3O4 and (Fe,Mn)3O4 phases form, supporting the conclusion that scale morphology is governed by manganese enrichment and interfacial aluminate growth.

Figure 20 presents a six-stage schematic diagram illustrating the growth mechanism of small manganese oxide nodules during oxidation of α and α+β-Mn Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 800°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr. Stage 1 shows void formation beneath the alumina scale. Stage 2 depicts flaw development in the overlying Al2O3 layer, allowing O2(g) to react with Mn(g) vapor from the substrate. Stage 3 illustrates outward growth of Mn2O3. Stage 4 shows filling of the void via short-circuit diffusion of O2− inward and Mn2+ outward, with Mn3O4 forming inside the void. Stage 5 depicts formation of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 particles at the oxide-metal interface as Al3+ diffuses inward. Stage 6 shows the completed nodule converted to Mn2O3 with a continuous healing layer of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the base. The figure supports the mechanism that nodule nucleation occurs at defects above substrate voids, with growth driven by manganese vapor reacting with oxygen, followed by ionic diffusion and internal aluminum oxidation to establish a protective healing layer that slows further growth.
